title: The British Journal of Sociology in the 2000s: sociology in a new century
abstract: The British Journal of Sociology entered its sixth decade along with a new century. It was a moment, as elsewhere, for millennial reflections – although sociologists were no longer much given to making predictions. Looking back at the Journal over the ten years since, it is not yet clear how the 2000s hold together as a period of sociological research and debate. John Urry, who wrote the introduction to the first issue of the decade, began by noting the ‘arbitrary date’ that provided the hook for the millennium special issue (Urry 2000: 1). Decades are another arbitrary way of carving up time, and at such close range the organizing themes are not obvious. This is partly to do, no doubt, with the fact that the contents of the BJS between 2000 and 2009 currently look like normal science – this is what sociologists do. It is only at a distance, as theories and methodologies develop (or go out of vogue), concepts are revised, arguments are won and lost, problems slip off or come onto the research agenda, that it is possible to make out the intellectual rhythm of a decade. With that caveat, some things are noticeable. The first is the retirement from the scene of sociology's founding thinkers. There is still an engagement with traditions of sociological thought, but much of the work of textual interpretation and argument that typified earlier decades seems, for now, to have been done. The relative anonymity of Marx, Durkheim and Weber in the Journal during this period is offset by efforts to extend the canon to include neglected (or discredited) thinkers: Thomas Wright, Adam Smith, Adam Ferguson, Herbert Spencer (see Barbalet 2005; Brewer 2007; Zafirovski 2000). Göran Therborn, in the 2000 issue, refers to ‘second century sociology’, but the discipline's pre-history seems to be getting longer. Meanwhile more recent thinkers – Luhmann, Foucault, and especially Bourdieu – provide important points of analytical reference without clearly capturing the field. Related to this first point, I would suggest, is the diversity and range of empirical work published in the Journal during this time. Such core disciplinary concerns as work, religion, state forms, gender, education, ethnicity, social mobility and inequality remain central. But a range of other issues also have a significant presence: sport (there is quite a lot of football); crime and violence; consumption (including binge-drinking and addiction); art and music; technology (cctv and the web, as well as actor-network theory); risk and the environment. A critical element of BJS during the 2000s has been a series of public lectures and published debates. These have provided a forum for international and interdisciplinary exchanges on key issues: notable among these have been the debates on public sociology in response first to Michael Burawoy's address on this topic at the 2004 American Sociological Association conference (see Burawoy 2005), and to Bryan Turner's (2006) piece on British sociology and public intellectuals. In the later part of the decade the BJS public lecture series generated critical debates around the arguments of such figures as Judith Butler, Robert J. Sampson, and Loïc Wacquant. Selecting two pieces published in the Journal during the 2000s was, I would argue, the most difficult editorial proposition for this anniversary issue. The impact of many of the articles from this period is still live, but it is harder to say (sociologists being wary of making predictions) what will endure in terms of critical influence or provocation. One of the recurring themes that has been apparent during this decade, however, in reflections on the discipline itself, is the changing way in which sociologists understand their object of study. The concept of ‘society’ as a frame that is more or less bounded, fairly stable, and implicitly national, has been put into serious question. The spaces of the social are harder to fix as units of analysis. International or transnational or post-national exchanges and relations produce complex sites of power, identity and interaction, such that the analysis of local practices or problems requires a grasp of how these are linked to other, sometimes very distant, places, players or processes. Two articles that have spoken very directly to this kind of sociological imagination are John Urry's work on ‘Mobile sociology’(2000) from the first issue of the decade, and Ulrich Beck and Natan Sznaider's (2006) ‘Unpacking cosmopolitanism for the social sciences: a research agenda’. These are pieces that have elicited sustained debate, in as well as outside the pages of the Journal. The contributions offered here by Caroline Knowles, Vincent Kaufmann, Yasemin Soysal and Nina Glick Schiller in this way can be seen as critical interventions in a debate that is current; their arguments suggest how such debates might or should move forward into the next decade of sociological analysis and argument.
